html:
The 3D Geometry of the Scene
Image-plane collinear points
Inset is rescaled to keep all four collinear points readable.
⚠ Figure failed to load

plan.contextChunk:
$\mathbf{g}_b$ is the projection of the desk height on the bookshelf.
However, due to the foreshortening effect induced by perspective
projection, we cannot simply rely on the ratio of image distances to
estimate the desk's true height. Instead, we will utilize the
cross-ratio invariant to make this estimation.



:::{.column-margin}
As J. J. Gibson pointed out, the ground plane is very
useful for all kinds of 3D scene measurements.
:::

In order to
use the cross-ratio invariant we need two sets of four colinear points
related by a projective geometry. @fig-office_measuring_desk_sketch
shows a sketch of the setup we have in @fig-office_measuring_desk.

![Projection of desk height onto the bookshelf. We start with the bottom and top image coordinates of points along vertical edges of the bookshelf: 
$\mathbf{t}_b = [702~~2{,}958]^\mathsf{T}$ 
$\mathbf{g}_b= [987~~618]^\mathsf{T}$ 
and the desk: 
$\mathbf{t}_d = [679~~1{,}018]^\mathsf{T}$ 
$\mathbf{g}_d= [793~~59]^\mathsf{T}$ 
We compute the points 
$\mathbf{a}= [4{,}471~~2{,}486]^\mathsf{T}$ 
and
$\mathbf{b}= [2{,}237~~2{,}765]^\mathsf{T}$ 
as described in the text.](figures/single_view_3d/office_measuring_desk.png){width="85%" #fig-office_measuring_desk}


In the image plane, we have the four image points $\mathbf{t}_b$,
$\mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{g}_b$ and $\mathbf{v}_3$. Those four 2D image
points correspond to the 3D locations given by the top of the bookshelf,
desk height, ground and infinity (which is the location of the vertical
vanishing point in 3D).

![Sketch of the setting from @fig-office_measuring_desk. There are two sets of colinear points related by central projection. Their cross-ratios are identical.](figures/single_view_3d/office_measuring_desk_sketch.png){width="70%" #fig-office_measuring_desk_sketch}

The rest of calculations requires computing distances between points,
which only works when using heterogeneous coordinates (or homogeneous
coordinates if the last component is equal to 1 for all points). The
cross-ratio invariant between both sets of four points gives as the
equality:

$$CR(\mathbf{P}_1,\mathbf{P}_2,\mathbf{P}_3,\mathbf{P}_4) = CR(\mathbf{p}_1,\mathbf{p}_2,\mathbf{p}_3,\mathbf{p}_4)
$${#eq-cross_ratio_shelf_desk}

The left side of the equality corresponds to the distances between the
3D points and the right side to the distances of the image points
measured in pixel units. As $\mathbf{P}_1$ is in infinity (as it
corresponds to the vanishing point), the left side ratio only has two
terms, resulting in the ratio between the bookshelf and desk heights. To

elements: Ground plane (semi-transparent blue rectangle representing the floor)
Vertical bookshelf pole (thick black vertical cylinder)
3D points on the pole: P2 (ground level), P3 (desk height), P4 (bookshelf top)
Dashed vertical line extending downwards representing the direction to infinity P1
Camera center (represented as a 3D focal point/pinhole)
Tilted image plane (flat rectangular frame positioned in front of the camera center)
Projection rays (dashed lines) from camera center to P2, P3, and P4
Parallel projection ray from camera center pointing vertically downwards (parallel to the pole) to find the vertical vanishing point v3 (p1)
Projected points on the image plane: p2 (gb), p3 (b), p4 (tb), and p1 (v3)
Projected line segment on the image plane connecting the projected points
On-screen 2D HUD or overlay text showing the computed ratios: Simple Ratio vs. Cross-Ratio
Dimension annotations: h_bookshelf (197cm) and h_desk

interactions.label: Desk height (h_desk)
interactions.teaches: Changing the desk height shifts P3 and its projection p3, showing how the perspective projection changes non-linearly

interactions.label: Camera Tilt (Pitch)
interactions.teaches: Tilting the camera shifts the vanishing point v3 and all projected points, demonstrating that while individual pixel distances warp, their cross-ratio remains perfectly invariant

interactions.label: Camera Height
interactions.teaches: Changing the camera elevation alters the perspective distortion on the image plane, showing the robustness of projective invariants under different viewpoints

demo_steps.title: The 3D Geometry of the Scene
demo_steps.narration: Welcome! Here we have a 3D bookshelf of height 197cm sitting on a ground plane. We mark key vertical points: P2 at the base, P4 at the top, and P3 at an unknown desk height. The camera projects these points onto a tilted image plane, while the vertical direction itself projects to a vanishing point v3 (p1) on the image plane.

demo_steps.title: Foreshortening Prevents Simple Ratios
demo_steps.narration: Notice how the desk height P3 projects to p3 on the image plane. Because of perspective foreshortening, the simple ratio of image distances, (p3 - p2) / (p4 - p2), does not equal the true physical ratio of heights. We cannot simply measure pixels directly to find the height.

demo_steps.title: The Cross-Ratio Invariant
demo_steps.narration: By introducing the vertical vanishing point v3 (p1), we get a fourth collinear point. The cross-ratio of these four projected points, CR(p1, p2, p3, p4), is mathematically identical to the 3D cross-ratio. Since P1 is at infinity, the 3D equation simplifies beautifully to express the exact ratio of the desk height to the bookshelf height.

demo_steps.title: Invariance Under Camera Tilt
demo_steps.narration: Watch what happens when we tilt the camera upwards. The image plane tilts, sending the vanishing point v3 (p1) far down and drastically stretching the projected points. Despite this extreme perspective distortion, the computed cross-ratio remains absolutely constant, proving its power for single-view metrology.

demo_steps.title: Changing Viewpoint Elevation
demo_steps.narration: Let's raise the camera higher. Even as the overall viewpoint changes and the image coordinates shift completely, the cross-ratio invariant continues to yield the exact same height of 75cm for the desk. This allows precise 3D measurements from a single, uncalibrated photograph!

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: A three-quarter perspective view from the side, positioned to clearly see the camera center on the left, the tilted image plane in the middle, and the vertical bookshelf pole on the right.
interactionPlan.notes: The simulation should dynamically compute the 3D projection of points P2, P3, P4, and the directional vector of P1 (vertical infinity) onto the virtual camera's image plane. Display a small 2D inset of the image plane alongside the 3D view, drawing the projected points p1, p2, p3, p4 and dynamically updating the calculated cross-ratio value to show it matches the 3D ratio (h_desk / 197) at all times.

attempts.html:
The 3D Geometry of the Scene
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: A 2D image-plane inset has been added which does not exist in the original figure, causing severe clutter and label overlapping on the right side
Labels such as h_bookshelf, h_desk, P2, and the inset labels are overlapping and colliding, making them unreadable
The camera center in the original is a simple black point with dashed line to v3, whereas here it has a generic sphere and tight helper lines
evaluation.notes: Excellent mathematical concept and interactivity, but severely degraded by the hallucinated 2D inset which causes massive label overlaps on the right.
action_items: Remove the hallucinated '2D image-plane inset' entirely to declutter the right side of the figure
Reposition the dimension labels (h_bookshelf and h_desk) further to the right to avoid overlapping with the pole and ground plane
Increase the size and spacing of the image plane labels (p1, p2, p3, p4) to improve readability
Adjust the camera target and initial zoom to frame the 3D scene more cleanly without label clipping

feedback.rationale: The concept and geometry are highly accurate, but the implementation hallucinated an unnecessary 2D inset and has overlapping labels, which are execution-level issues that the generator can resolve.
action_items: Remove the hallucinated '2D image-plane inset' entirely to declutter the right side of the figure
Reposition the dimension labels (h_bookshelf and h_desk) further to the right to avoid overlapping with the pole and ground plane
Increase the size and spacing of the image plane labels (p1, p2, p3, p4) to improve readability
Adjust the camera target and initial zoom to frame the 3D scene more cleanly without label clipping
actionItems: Remove the hallucinated '2D image-plane inset' entirely to declutter the right side of the figure
Reposition the dimension labels (h_bookshelf and h_desk) further to the right to avoid overlapping with the pole and ground plane
Increase the size and spacing of the image plane labels (p1, p2, p3, p4) to improve readability
Adjust the camera target and initial zoom to frame the 3D scene more cleanly without label clipping

attempts.html:
The 3D Geometry of the Scene
Image-plane collinear points
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The projected points p2, p3, p4 on the image plane are very tightly clustered, causing their text labels to overlap and become unreadable
The vanishing point label p1=v3 is partially obscured behind the HTML narration box
The 3D label P3 slightly overlaps with the vertical measurement line for h_bookshelf
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive demonstration of the cross-ratio metrology concept with high mathematical accuracy, but suffers from some overlapping label clutter in 3D.
action_items: Increase the spacing or adjust the default camera zoom/angle so that the projected points p2, p3, and p4 on the tilted image plane do not overlap as heavily
Shift the canvas or adjust the position of the vanishing point p1=v3 to prevent it from rendering underneath the UI narration panel
Adjust the label offsets for P2, P3, and P4 to prevent overlaps with the double-headed height measurement arrows

feedback.rationale: The conceptual representation and interactive features are highly accurate, but minor layout adjustments and label offsets are needed to resolve text overlaps.
action_items: Increase the spacing or adjust the default camera zoom/angle so that the projected points p2, p3, and p4 on the tilted image plane do not overlap as heavily
Shift the canvas or adjust the position of the vanishing point p1=v3 to prevent it from rendering underneath the UI narration panel
Adjust the label offsets for P2, P3, and P4 to prevent overlaps with the double-headed height measurement arrows

Despite this extreme perspective distortion, the computed cross-ratio remains absolutely constant, proving its power for single-view metrology.

demo_steps.narration: Let's raise the camera higher. Even as the overall viewpoint changes and the image coordinates shift completely, the cross-ratio invariant continues to yield the exact same height of 75cm for the desk. This allows precise 3D measurements from a single, uncalibrated photograph!

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: A three-quarter perspective view from the side, positioned to clearly see the camera center on the left, the tilted image plane in the middle, and the vertical bookshelf pole on the right.
interactionPlan.notes: The simulation should dynamically compute the 3D projection of points P2, P3, P4, and the directional vector of P1 (vertical infinity) onto the virtual camera's image plane. Display a small 2D inset of the image plane alongside the 3D view, drawing the projected points p1, p2, p3, p4 and dynamically updating the calculated cross-ratio value to show it matches the 3D ratio (h_desk / 197) at all times.

discrepancies: Labels on the image plane (p2, p3, p4) overlap heavily, making them difficult to read
Red dashed leader lines are added to connect image points to their text labels, creating visual clutter not present in the original
The 'Ground plane' label overlaps with the P2 point label
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive demonstration of single-view metrology and cross-ratio invariance, but suffers from label overlapping on the image plane.
action_items: Adjust the label offsets for p2, p3, and p4 on the image plane to spread them out and prevent overlapping
Move the 'Ground plane' label slightly forward or downward to avoid overlapping with the P2 point label
Reduce the visual prominence or remove the red dashed leader lines to clean up the image plane visualization
Slightly increase the font size of the image plane labels for better readability

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and geometry are highly accurate (scores of 5), but there are label overlapping and visual clutter issues on the image plane that require precise rendering adjustments by the generator.
